title: Spring Security 6 Readiness Knowledge Base
summary: Guidance for using EMP Spring Security 6 readiness evidence during Spring Security 5.x to 6.x modernization planning.
guide: Start with applicability. The Spring Security 6 readiness pack is useful only when Spring Security dependencies or configuration code are detected.

Separate security migration from general Boot migration. Jakarta namespace work, Spring Boot 3 compatibility, authorization DSL changes, and method security changes should be tracked as related but distinct workstreams.
Treat static security findings as triage evidence. They identify configuration areas that need review before a consultant promises upgrade execution.

risks.name: WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter usage
risks.whyItMatters: WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter is removed in modern Spring Security configuration styles.
risks.recommendation: Replace adapter-based configuration with SecurityFilterChain beans and validate compile/test evidence.

risks.name: Legacy matcher APIs
risks.whyItMatters: antMatchers, mvcMatchers, and regexMatchers usage usually needs explicit request matching review.
risks.recommendation: Review URL matching semantics before replacing matcher APIs.

risks.name: authorizeRequests configuration
risks.whyItMatters: Authorization DSL changes can alter request authorization behavior if migrated mechanically.
risks.recommendation: Review authorization rules with compile, test, and route-level validation evidence.

risks.name: Global method security
risks.whyItMatters: Method security annotation changes can affect service authorization behavior.
risks.recommendation: Review method security configuration and protect it with focused tests.

risks.name: javax and Jakarta overlap
risks.whyItMatters: Spring Security 6 work usually happens alongside Spring Boot 3 and Jakarta namespace migration.
risks.recommendation: Track namespace work separately so security behavior risk is not hidden behind import rewrites.

validationNotes: Catalog-backed reports prove the report shape and risk vocabulary, not checkout compile success.
Security configuration changes need focused tests for authorization behavior, not only successful compilation.
Passing compile/test validation increases confidence, but route-level and method-level access behavior still needs project-specific review.
Failed validation evidence is useful when it honestly records build compatibility, runtime, dependency, or test environment blockers.

benchmarks.name: Spring Security Samples Legacy
benchmarks.note: Security sample material with matcher and authorization configuration patterns.

benchmarks.name: Spring Petclinic REST Security
benchmarks.note: Spring Boot 2.x application with Spring Security and Jakarta migration overlap.

benchmarks.name: Spring Boot RealWorld Security
benchmarks.note: JWT-oriented Spring Security usage in a Spring Boot 2.x application.

benchmarks.name: JHipster Sample Security
benchmarks.note: Generated application security configuration with authorization and method security concerns.

benchmarks.name: Keycloak Security Adapter
benchmarks.note: Security integration evidence with framework and Jakarta readiness overlap.

limits: The readiness report does not prove authorization behavior is preserved.
The readiness report does not replace route-level access testing.
The readiness report does not guarantee method security semantics are unchanged.
The readiness report does not apply Spring Security source transformations.
